medications.indication_rationale: Reduces inflammation, pain and healing time in aphthous ulcers; source prescribed triamcinolone 0.1% dental paste 2–3 times daily after meals and at bedtime.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply gently to ulcers 2–3 times daily after meals and at bedtime (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Short-term topical analgesia to enable drinking/eating; source applied 2% viscous lidocaine to ulcer beds. Use with care re: numbing and swallowing.
medications.dose_route_frequency: applied sparingly to ulcers before drinking/eating (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Background analgesia; source advised 1000mg every 6 hours.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally every 6 hours (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: No HSV features (no vesicles, keratinised mucosa spared) — antivirals are not indicated for aphthous ulcers.
Aphthous ulceration is not a bacterial infection; antibiotics are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a wide-bore straw placed past the front teeth to bypass the ulcers when drinking; apply topical anaesthetic before fluids.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mechanically separates fluid from ulcerated mucosa so hydration can be maintained.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid acidic, spicy, salty and rough/crunchy foods; prefer bland, cool foods and fluids.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These chemically/mechanically irritate ulcers and worsen pain.

patient_education_points.point: These are mouth ulcers (aphthous ulcers) — an inflammatory, non-infectious problem that tends to recur with stress. They heal on their own in 1–2 weeks; the steroid paste speeds healing and eases pain.
Keep drinking: use topical numbing gel and a straw past your front teeth so you can take fluids without touching the ulcers. Dab (don't rub) the steroid paste on and let it form a film.

Go to ED if you can't keep fluids down and stop passing urine or feel very lightheaded. See your GP if a single ulcer lasts more than 3 weeks (it needs to be checked), or to test for iron/B12/folate if these keep recurring.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Healing; arrange haematinics if frequently recurrent; biopsy any ulcer >3 weeks.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Cannot maintain hydration, a single ulcer persisting >3 weeks, or systemic features — ED / GP.

must_include_items: Topical corticosteroid (± topical anaesthetic) for aphthae
Analgesia-enabled hydration strategy (numbing + straw) with dehydration safety-net
acceptable_alternatives: Chlorhexidine or benzydamine mouthwash as adjuncts; topical anaesthetic gels
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antivirals (treating as HSV) or antibiotics
Dismissing the dehydration risk in a patient living alone with no intake
Recommending irritant mouthwashes (alcohol-based) that worsen pain
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires topical aphthous management, an analgesia-to-hydrate plan with dehydration safety-net, and the >3-week persistent-ulcer safety-net.
